entries.falsifiableTrigger: Two consecutive quarters where NVIDIA datacenter revenue (D2) falls sequentially while stated lead times shorten.
entries.mechanism: Buyers order more compute than vendors can build because scarce memory and packaging cap how many accelerators ship each quarter.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether hyperscaler spending stays high and whether memory supply catches up to orders.
entries.statement: Datacenter GPU demand continues to outrun NVIDIA's ability to serve it.
entries.title: NVDA demand durability

entries.falsifiableTrigger: TSMC CoWoS supply-demand gap (leadTimes) widening back above 20% or HBM order books (S10) clearing before 2028.
entries.mechanism: High-bandwidth memory stays sold out while packaging lines keep adding capacity, so memory now sets the ceiling on how many accelerators reach buyers.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether memory makers can lift high-bandwidth output to meet 2027 to 2028 demand.
entries.statement: High-bandwidth memory, not advanced packaging, is now the supply that caps how much merchant-GPU revenue vendors can book. Packaging is easing as the TSMC CoWoS gap narrows from about 20% to 10% by end 2026 and ASE and Hanmi add capacity, and NVIDIA's supply commitments run to $119.0B-$145B, while HBM3E/HBM4 stay sold out through 2027 with demand far above supply.
entries.title: Supply constraint binding

entries.falsifiableTrigger: AMD Instinct share of the AI accelerator market (market-share-pct) failing to rise above 7% through 2026, or AMD datacenter revenue (D2) growth stalling for two quarters.
entries.mechanism: Large multi-year commitments from Meta and Rackspace plus a maturing Instinct line give buyers a real second supplier they actually deploy.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether announced design wins convert into shipped share rather than staying paper commitments.
entries.statement: AMD converts its Instinct roadmap into material datacenter share.
entries.title: AMD credible second source

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Custom-ASIC share of AI-chip shipments (market-share-pct) stalling below 27.8% or its 44.6% growth rate falling under merchant-GPU growth in 2026.
entries.mechanism: Hyperscalers building their own chips grow those shipments far faster than merchant GPUs, so they take a rising share of new AI compute.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether custom chips stay locked to their makers or become broadly rentable.
entries.statement: Hyperscaler custom silicon meaningfully displaces merchant-GPU demand growth.
entries.title: Custom ASIC substitution

entries.falsifiableTrigger: H100 rental $/GPU-hr (D6) or secondary resale (gpuSpotPrice) falling further quarter over quarter as Blackwell supply ramps.
entries.mechanism: As new Blackwell and Rubin supply reaches the market, older H100 rental rates drift down toward commodity levels.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to how fast new-generation supply floods the rental market.
entries.statement: GPU rental/spot price levels hold up despite added supply.
entries.title: Pricing power persistence

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA China revenue (exportControlExposure) recovering above $9,000M in a single quarter, signaling the constraint no longer binds.
entries.mechanism: US license rules and tariffs block or tax the largest China datacenter GPU sales, cutting the market vendors can legally serve.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether case-by-case licenses actually convert into shipped China revenue.
entries.statement: Export-control policy materially constrains addressable merchant-GPU demand.
entries.title: Export control exposure

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA vendor-financed GPU commitments (rpoBacklog) climbing beyond the ~210,000 units across Firmus and Sharon AI in coming quarters.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA lends money and guarantees to rent back idle GPUs so smaller clouds can buy its chips, which books demand that leans on NVIDIA's own credit.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the financed clouds earn enough rental income to repay.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's own revenue-sharing and credit-support financing increasingly underwrites its accelerator demand, coupling reported demand strength to NVIDIA-extended credit and adding circularity risk to buildout durability.
entries.title: Vendor-financed demand circularity

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Top-customer share of NVIDIA revenue (customerConcentration) falling below the prior 12/11/11 pattern, or the top three dropping under 21/17/16 percent.
entries.mechanism: A handful of large buyers make up over half of sales, so any one pulling back would dent revenue sharply.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the largest customers shift orders to their own in-house silicon.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's data-center revenue is increasingly concentrated in a few direct customers, raising demand-durability risk if any single hyperscaler slows orders or shifts to in-house silicon.
entries.title: Customer concentration risk

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA losing its number-one datacenter Ethernet switch revenue rank (market-share-pct) within a year, or Spectrum-X attach to GPU sales falling quarter over quarter.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA sells its switches together with its GPUs, so buying its accelerators pulls in its networking and raises the cost of switching away.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to open Ethernet alternatives eroding the bundled networking advantage.
entries.statement: NVIDIA now leads the data-center Ethernet switch market and sells that networking bundled with its GPUs, deepening a full-system moat that raises switching costs versus standalone accelerators and custom chips. NVIDIA took the top revenue spot in data-center Ethernet switching in Q1 2026 by pairing Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300, and its NVLink systems cut cost per token, but no finding compares networking growth against compute growth, so the sharper claim that networking scales faster than compute is not supported.
entries.title: Networking attach as systems moat

entries.falsifiableTrigger: A new NVIDIA generation failing to lower cost per token (flopsPerDollar), or AMD's per-dollar hardware edge translating into inference share gains within a year.
entries.mechanism: Each new generation cuts the cost to serve a token, so more inference jobs become affordable to run on NVIDIA hardware.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to AMD's hardware price edge converting into real inference deployments.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's each-generation drop in cost per token pulls more inference workloads onto its GPUs and widens the market it can serve.
entries.title: Cheaper inference pulls in demand

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Any of the 16 take-or-pay HBM contracts (rpoBacklog) being cancelled or deposits refunded within the 5-year term.
entries.mechanism: Buyers put billions in nonrefundable deposits behind multi-year memory contracts, so they are committed to take the chips those parts go into.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the deposits are truly nonrefundable if demand softens.
entries.statement: Non-cancelable multi-year HBM contracts backed by billions in customer deposits lock a large share of future accelerator demand into place and reduce the chance that today's buildout is speculative.
entries.title: Prepaid HBM take-or-pay locks in accelerator demand

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Approved China licenses (exportControlExposure) lifting China revenue above the sub-50% cap, or the case-by-case window reverting to denial within two quarters.
entries.mechanism: Regulators approve China sales one deal at a time under a hard ceiling, so access stays partial and slow.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether case-by-case approvals actually turn into shipped revenue.
entries.statement: China datacenter compute is reopening to NVIDIA only through case-by-case H200- and MI325-class licenses capped under half of comparable US sales, with Chinese buyers now signaling first purchases. This cycle adds H200-class licensing shifted from denial to case-by-case and buyer interest from Alibaba, ByteDance and DeepSeek, broadening the earlier MI325-only picture, while China revenue sits at $4,550M and H200 licenses have booked no revenue yet.
entries.title: Conditional China re-opening with a capped ceiling

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Additional frontier-scale models (S9) trained on non-NVIDIA accelerators within a year, or AMD ROCm design wins (releaseCadence) accelerating.
entries.mechanism: Rewriting AI software to run well on other chips is costly, so buyers with a choice tend to stay on NVIDIA even when rivals are cheaper.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to how fast ROCm and other alternative software stacks mature.
entries.statement: The difficulty of running existing AI software on non-Nvidia chips keeps customers on Nvidia even when rivals cut hardware prices.
entries.title: CUDA software lock-in sustains the moat

entries.falsifiableTrigger: AI-server memory bill-of-materials cost per S10 falls back toward baseline for 2 consecutive quarters, or DRAM output restrictions ease.
entries.mechanism: Memory and storage chips are scarce and their prices climb weekly, so the parts bill for each AI server keeps rising.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether memory and storage supply loosens.
entries.statement: Fast-rising memory and NVMe prices are pushing up what it costs to build each AI server, which could strain buyer budgets and squeeze system margins.
entries.title: Rising memory costs inflate AI-server economics

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Any of Vera Rubin, MI450/Helios, Trainium 3 or Google TPU (releaseCadence) slipping mass production past 2H 2026, or HBM (leadTimes) throttling the combined ramp.
entries.mechanism: Four major vendors bring their newest accelerators to volume production in the same half-year, adding more total supply than any single ramp would.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether memory supply gates the combined ramp.
entries.statement: A simultaneous mass-production ramp of NVIDIA, AMD, AWS, and Google accelerators is set to expand total accelerator supply in the second half of 2026.
entries.title: Multi-vendor accelerator supply wave in second-half 2026

entries.falsifiableTrigger: AMD customer concentration (customerConcentration) easing to a broad base, or one large deal cancellation cutting datacenter revenue (D2) in a quarter.
entries.mechanism: A few very large orders like Meta and Rackspace drive most of AMD's accelerator growth, so its results swing with a handful of partners.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to any single mega-partner delaying or cancelling its order.
entries.statement: AMD's data-center accelerator growth depends on a small set of very large commitments, which concentrates both its upside and its downside in a handful of partners.
entries.title: AMD growth leans on a few mega-deals

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Crescent Island (S9) missing its 2H 2026 sampling, or its LPDDR5X design still requiring the same advanced packaging capacity as HBM parts.
entries.mechanism: Using standard memory lets an inference chip skip the scarce advanced-packaging and high-bandwidth-memory steps that constrain other accelerators.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether Crescent Island hits its sampling date and wins real inference workloads.
entries.statement: Intel's Crescent Island inference GPU uses standard LPDDR5X memory instead of high-bandwidth memory to sidestep the advanced-packaging and memory bottleneck, opening an alternative supply route for inference chips.
entries.title: Memory-based inference chips route around the packaging bottleneck

entries.falsifiableTrigger: TSMC wafer or CoWoS capacity (S10) running slack, or foundry prices flat to down over two quarters despite steady AI orders.
entries.mechanism: AI orders now come from many chip types at once, so they fill TSMC's lines across nodes and let it hold firm on price.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the breadth of AI orders holds if any one segment slows.
entries.statement: AI demand spreading across GPUs, custom ASICs, networking and power chips fills TSMC's capacity across nodes and lets it raise prices.
entries.title: Broadening AI demand tightens TSMC capacity and pricing

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Hyperscaler AI capex (D2) growth reversing from the 67% jump toward $650B, or order cancellations appearing within two quarters.
entries.mechanism: If the sharp jump in AI spending fails to pay off, hyperscalers cut budgets and reduce chip orders.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to evidence over the next few quarters on whether AI spending earns a return.
entries.statement: Hyperscalers' sharp jump in AI capital spending risks a pullback if returns disappoint, which would cut accelerator orders.
entries.title: Hyperscaler AI spending may outrun near-term returns

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA non-GAAP gross margin (grossMargin) falling below 70% for two consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: Strong demand and software lock-in let NVIDIA charge premium prices, holding its gross margin near three-quarters while rivals earn far less per chip.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to competitive pricing pressure from AMD and custom chips.
entries.statement: NVIDIA keeps its gross margin near three-quarters even while ramping new products, far above rivals, showing buyers still pay premium prices.
entries.title: NVIDIA holds a premium gross margin as it ramps new chips

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Top three CoWoS clients' share (S10) dropping below 70% within a year, or CoWoS lead times falling under 20 weeks.
entries.mechanism: Because three customers book most packaging slots, the packaging line's fortunes rise and fall with just those buyers' orders.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether NVIDIA and AMD orders hold through the ramp.
entries.statement: A few AI chip buyers control most of TSMC's advanced-packaging output, so any one of them cutting orders would leave capacity that is hard to refill.
entries.title: AI packaging supply is concentrated in a few buyers
